mappings.rationaleAI: Medicine from Hippocratic traditions through modern biomedicine covers the full history of medicine (A), including Greco-Roman medicine (A.1.d), Renaissance (A.1.f), 17th century (A.1.g), 18th century (A.1.h), 19th century (A.2), and 20th century advances (A.3).
Epidemiological methods relate to public health (B.2.d) and nonclinical specialties (B.3).
Pandemic diseases relate to immunology advances (A.3.b), public health (B.2.d), and tropical medicine (B.2.h).
Plague relates to medieval medicine (A.1.e), immunology (A.3.b), and public health (B.2.d).
Pathogen dynamics relate to bacteriology (A.2.c), chemotherapy (A.3.a), and immunology (A.3.b).
HIV/AIDS relates to chemotherapy advances (A.3.a), public health (B.2.d), and tropical medicine (B.2.h).
Viral biology addresses vaccines for viral diseases (A.3.b.ii).
Bacteria as disease agents relate to bacteriology (A.2.c) and chemotherapy (A.3.a).
Immune system biology addresses immunology advances (A.3.b) and immunology as specialty (B.2.f).
Cancer biology addresses cancer research (A.3.d.ii).
Pharmacology relates to chemotherapy (A.3.a) and pharmacology as specialty (B.3).
Anaesthesia relates to anesthesia discovery (A.2.d), surgery importance (A.4.a), inhalation procedures (A.4.e), and anesthesiology (B.1.h).
Anatomy relates to Greco-Roman discoveries (A.1.d) and Renaissance improvements (A.1.f.i).
Physiology relates to advances in physiology (A.2.b) and medical physiology (B.3).
Endocrinology relates to endocrinology developments (A.3.c) and endocrinology specialty (B.2.e).
Nutrition addresses deficiency diseases (A.3.d.i) and nutrition specialty (B.3).
Public health addresses public health specialty (B.2.d).
Psychiatry addresses psychiatry specialty (B.1.g).
Clinical psychology relates to psychiatry specialty (B.1.g).
Psychotherapy relates to psychiatry specialty (B.1.g).
CBT relates to psychiatry specialty (B.1.g).
Schizophrenia relates to psychiatry (B.1.g).
Depression and bipolar disorder relate to psychiatry (B.1.g).
Anxiety disorders relate to psychiatry (B.1.g).
Autism relates to neurology (B.1.f) and psychiatry (B.1.g).
Addiction relates to psychiatry (B.1.g) and public health (B.2.d).
Psychopathy relates to psychiatry (B.1.g).
Dementia relates to neurology (B.1.f) and gerontology (B.3).
Dyslexia relates to neurology (B.1.f).
The brain addresses neurology (B.1.f).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationaleAI: Sleep biology relates to neurology (B.1.f) and medical physiology (B.3).
Pain management relates to chemotherapy advances (A.3.a) and anesthesiology (B.1.h).
Aging addresses gerontology (B.3).
Cell biology relates to cellular pathology as a 19th-century doctrine (A.2.a).
Blood biology relates to blood transfusions (A.4.d) and pathology (B.1.h).
Dental health relates to dentistry (C.1).
Medical ethics relates to medical jurisprudence (B.2.b).
Medical law relates to medical jurisprudence (B.2.b).
Forensic science relates to medical jurisprudence (B.2.b).
Veterinary medicine relates to public health (B.2.d).
Microscopy relates to microscope importance in medicine (A.1.g.i), medical technology (A.3.d.iv), and ancillary disciplines (B.4).
Radioactivity's biomedical applications relate to X-ray discovery (A.2.e), medical technology (A.3.d.iv), and radiology (B.1.a).
Circadian disruption health consequences relate to medical physiology (B.3).
Molecular science relating to pharmacology connects to nonclinical specialties (B.3).
Covers scientific and medical practices in ancient Greece and Rome including inquiry into human health, directly addressing systematic medicine in the Greco-Roman world and the work of Hippocrates.
Covers chemical synthesis and the rise of organic chemistry, relevant to advances in chemotherapy and the discovery of antibiotics and synthetic drugs in treating bacterial diseases.
Covers stem cell therapeutic potential for treating diseases including diabetes and Parkinson's, relevant to the emergence of surgical specialties and nonclinical medical sciences.
Covers Vesalius and Harvey alongside emergence of new investigative methods, directly relevant to Renaissance medicine and 17th-century discoveries including circulation of the blood.
Covers DNA structure discovery, gene expression, and pharmaceutical applications, relevant to new doctrines including cellular pathology and nonclinical medical specialties.
Covers natural selection and practical implications such as antibiotic resistance, directly relevant to new 19th-century doctrines including natural selection that shaped scientific medicine.
